concernsHelp.title: Common Concerns & Help
concernsHelp.description: Worried about risks? Get honest answers to the questions banks won't ask.

items.question: What is a HELOC?
items.answer: A Home Equity Line of Credit (HELOC) is a revolving credit line secured by your home's equity. It works like a credit card - you can borrow, repay, and borrow again during the draw period (typically 10 years), followed by a repayment period (typically 20 years).

items.question: How is HELOC different from a Home Equity Loan?
items.answer: A HELOC is a revolving line of credit with variable interest rates, while a Home Equity Loan is a lump sum with fixed rates. HELOCs offer more flexibility - you only pay interest on what you borrow, and you can access funds multiple times during the draw period.

items.question: How do you calculate the maximum credit line?
items.answer: Maximum Credit Line = (Home Value × 85%) - Current Mortgage Balance. Most lenders allow you to borrow up to 85% of your home's value, minus what you still owe on your mortgage.

items.question: What are LTV and DTI?
items.answer: LTV (Loan-to-Value) is the ratio of your total debt to home value. DTI (Debt-to-Income) is your monthly debt payments divided by monthly income. Lenders typically prefer LTV below 85% and DTI below 43%.

items.question: How is HELOC interest rate determined?
items.answer: HELOC rates are typically Prime Rate + Margin. The Prime Rate fluctuates with market conditions (currently around 8.5%), and lenders add a margin (typically 0.5-2%) based on your credit score and financial profile.

items.question: What are the risks of using a HELOC?
items.answer: Main risks include: variable interest rates that can increase your payments, using your home as collateral (risk of foreclosure if you can't pay), and the temptation to overspend. Always borrow responsibly and have a repayment plan.

items.question: Is HELOC interest tax-deductible?
items.answer: Yes, but only if you use the funds to buy, build, or substantially improve your home (IRS Publication 936). Interest on funds used for other purposes (like debt consolidation or vacations) is not tax-deductible.

items.question: What are Draw Period and Repayment Period?
items.answer: Draw Period (typically 10 years): You can borrow and repay flexibly, paying only interest. Repayment Period (typically 20 years): You can no longer borrow, and must repay both principal and interest with fixed monthly payments.

items.question: What if my report download failed or I lost the download link?
items.answer: Don't worry! You can retrieve your report anytime using your Stripe Transaction ID. After payment, Stripe sends you an official receipt with a Transaction ID (format: pi_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx). Visit our Retrieve Reports page, enter your Transaction ID, and you'll get a fresh download link valid for 24 hours. Your reports are safely stored and accessible whenever you need them.

footer.disclaimer: Disclaimer: This tool and calculations are for estimation and educational purposes only. The figures provided do not constitute professional financial, legal, or tax advice. Actual HELOC terms are determined by lenders and may differ from estimates. Users should consult with qualified professionals before making any financial decisions.
